topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the printed sheet, the fluorescent lights of the clinic room seeming to intensify the stark white of the paper. Her hands, which had been trembling slightly as she held the file, began to shake more noticeably. The names were wrong. The diagnoses, the medications—they belonged to Mr. Harrison, a man she’d never met, not even in the waiting room. A slow, hot flush crept up her neck, starting at her collarbone and spreading across her cheeks. She quickly tried to shove the papers into her tote bag, but the movement felt clumsy, every muscle in her body suddenly tense. When Dr. Chen finally called her name, Elara managed a tight, breathy reply, her gaze fixed firmly on the linoleum floor, hoping the silence would somehow absorb the sudden, overwhelming heat rising within her chest.

[story 2]

Mark reread the discharge summary for the third time, his brow furrowed deep enough to create canyons between his eyes. The details—the surgical notes, the prescribed dosages—were all alien, belonging to a patient whose circumstances he couldn't fathom. A deep, cold knot tightened in his stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. He instinctively pulled his shoulders inward, hunching slightly as if trying to shrink away from the scrutiny of the room. When the nurse approached to confirm the information, Mark’s voice came out unnaturally high and thin. "Yes," he mumbled, focusing intently on a smudge on the desk, desperately wishing the floor would swallow him whole.

[story 3]

When Sarah finally connected the dots—the peculiar allergy notations, the strangely specific lab results—the realization hit her with a sickening lurch. She dropped the clipboard she was holding; the sound echoed loudly in the quiet hallway. Her face felt instantly hot, the color draining from her skin as if someone had pulled a plug. She began fiddling incessantly with the frayed edge of her sleeve, twisting the fabric until her knuckles turned white. She couldn't meet anyone's eyes, opting instead to stare intently at the scuff marks on the wall, wishing with a sudden, sharp intensity that she could simply evaporate from the spot.

Her hands, which had been trembling slightly as she held the file, began to shake more noticeably. The names were wrong. The diagnoses, the medications—they belonged to Mr. Harrison, a man she’d never met, not even in the waiting room. A slow, hot flush crept up her neck, starting at her collarbone and spreading across her cheeks. When Dr. Chen finally called her name, Elara managed a tight, breathy reply, her gaze fixed firmly on the linoleum floor, hoping the silence would somehow absorb the sudden, overwhelming heat rising within her chest.

Mark reread the discharge summary for the third time, his brow furrowed deep enough to create canyons between his eyes. The details—the surgical notes, the prescribed dosages—were all alien, belonging to a patient whose circumstances he couldn't fathom. A deep, cold knot tightened in his stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. He instinctively pulled his shoulders inward, hunching slightly as if trying to shrink away from the scrutiny of the room. When the nurse approached to confirm the information, Mark’s voice came out unnaturally high and thin. "Yes," he mumbled, focusing intently on a smudge on the desk, desperately wishing the floor would swallow him whole.

When Sarah finally connected the dots—the peculiar allergy notations, the strangely specific lab results—the realization hit her with a sickening lurch. She dropped the clipboard she was holding; the sound echoed loudly in the quiet hallway. Her face felt instantly hot, the color draining from her skin as if someone had pulled a plug. She began fiddling incessantly with the frayed edge of her sleeve, twisting the fabric until her knuckles turned white. She couldn't meet anyone's eyes, opting instead to stare intently at the scuff marks on the wall, wishing with a sudden, sharp intensity that she could simply evaporate from the spot.
